content: Korean Sentence Structure (SOV): Korean follows Subject-Object-Verb order. Example: 저는 밥을 먹어요 (jeo-neun bap-eul meog-eo-yo) = 'I rice eat' = 'I eat rice.' The verb always comes at the end. Particles mark the subject (은/는, 이/가) and object (을/를). Time expressions come early: 저는 아침에 밥을 먹어요 (I eat rice in the morning).

은/는 vs 이/가 (Topic vs Subject Markers): 은/는 marks the topic (what the sentence is about, often contrastive). 이/가 marks the subject (who/what performs the action, neutral). Examples: 저는 학생이에요 (As for me, I'm a student — topic), 제가 할게요 (I'll do it — emphasizing the subject). Use 은 after consonant: 책은, use 는 after vowel: 저는. Use 이 after consonant: 책이, use 가 after vowel: 학교가.

을/를 (Object Particle): Marks the direct object of a verb. 을 after consonant: 밥을 먹어요 (eat rice), 를 after vowel: 커피를 마셔요 (drink coffee). Can be dropped in casual speech: 밥 먹었어? = 'Did you eat?' (colloquial). Never use 을/를 with 이다 (to be) and 아니다 (to not be) — these use 이/가 instead.

이다 (To Be): Attached directly to nouns. 학생이다 = 'am/is/are a student.' Formal: 학생입니다, Polite: 학생이에요 (after consonant) / 학생예요 (after vowel), Casual: 학생이야. Negative 아니다: 학생이 아닙니다 (not a student). Question form: 학생입니까? / 학생이에요? Note the 이에요 vs 예요 distinction: 물이에요 (it's water — consonant ending), 사과예요 (it's an apple — vowel ending).

있다 / 없다 (To Exist / Not Exist): 있다 = there is / to have. 없다 = there isn't / to not have. Examples: 책이 있어요 (There is a book / I have a book), 시간이 없어요 (I don't have time). Location: 학교에 있어요 (I'm at school). Also used for adjectives describing existence: 재미있다 = interesting (has fun), 맛있다 = delicious (has taste), 멋있다 = cool/stylish (has style).

에 (Location/Time Particle): Marks location of existence (with 있다/없다), destination (with 가다/오다), or time. Examples: 학교에 가요 (I go to school), 집에 있어요 (I'm at home), 3시에 만나요 (Let's meet at 3). Note: 에서 marks location of action (where an action takes place): 학교에서 공부해요 (I study at school).

Korean Verb Conjugation — Present Tense: All Korean verbs end in -다 in dictionary form. Conjugate by removing -다 and adding: 아요/어요 (polite present). If the last vowel is 아 or 오 → 아요: 가다 → 가요 (go), 보다 → 봐요 (see), 오다 → 와요 (come). All other vowels → 어요: 먹다 → 먹어요 (eat), 읽다 → 읽어요 (read), 마시다 → 마셔요 (drink — 시 + 어 = 셔). 하다 verbs → 해요: 공부하다 → 공부해요 (study).

Korean Verb Conjugation — Past Tense: Add 았어요/었어요 after the verb stem. If last vowel is 아 or 오 → 았어요: 가다 → 갔어요 (went), 보다 → 봤어요 (saw). All others → 었어요: 먹다 → 먹었어요 (ate), 읽다 → 읽었어요 (read). 하다 → 했어요: 공부하다 → 공부했어요 (studied). Examples: 어제 뭐 했어요? (What did you do yesterday?), 영화를 봤어요 (I watched a movie).

Korean Verb Conjugation — Future Tense: Use -(으)ㄹ 거예요. Add 을 after consonant: 먹을 거예요 (will eat), 읽을 거예요 (will read). Add ㄹ after vowel: 갈 거예요 (will go), 볼 거예요 (will see). 하다 → 할 거예요: 공부할 거예요 (will study). Special cases: ㄹ-verbs drop the ㄹ before adding: 살다 → 살 거예요 (will live, not 살을 거예요), 놀다 → 놀 거예요 (will play).

Numbers: Korean has two number systems. Sino-Korean (일, 이, 삼, 사, 오, 육, 칠, 팔, 구, 십) used for dates, money, phone numbers, minutes. Native Korean (하나, 둘, 셋, 넷, 다섯, 여섯, 일곱, 여덟, 아홉, 열) used for counting items/people/animals, hours, age. Counting: 한 개 (1 thing), 두 명 (2 people), 세 마리 (3 animals), 네 시 (4 o'clock). Counter words: 개 (general), 명/분 (people — 분 more respectful), 마리 (animals), 권 (books), 장 (flat things), 병 (bottles), 잔 (cups).

도 (Also/Too): Replaces subject/object particles. 저도 학생이에요 (I'm a student too), 한국어도 배워요 (I learn Korean too). Can mark almost any noun: 어제도 비가 왔어요 (It rained yesterday too), 여기서도 공부해요 (I study here too). Not used with 은/는 in basic 'also' meaning — 도 replaces them.

하고 / (이)랑 / 와/과 (And/With): 하고 = and/with (most casual, most common in speech): 사과하고 바나나 (apples and bananas), 친구하고 영화를 봤어요 (I watched a movie with a friend). (이)랑 = and/with (colloquial): 사과랑 바나나. 와/과 = and/with (formal/written): 사과와 바나나 (와 after vowel), 책과 연필 (과 after consonant). Can connect nouns only, not clauses.

그리고 / 그래서 / 그런데 (Sentence Connectors): 그리고 = and/and then (simply adds info): 학교에 가요. 그리고 공부해요. (I go to school. And I study.) 그래서 = so/therefore (cause-effect): 배가 고파요. 그래서 밥을 먹어요. (I'm hungry. So I eat.) 그런데 = but/by the way (contrast or topic shift): 날씨가 추워요. 그런데 나가야 돼요. (The weather is cold. But I have to go out.)

못 / -지 못하다 (Cannot): 못 + verb = cannot (due to circumstances): 못 가요 (I can't go), 못 먹어요 (I can't eat). Verb stem + 지 못하다 = same meaning but more formal: 가지 못해요. 안 + verb = don't/doesn't (by choice): 안 가요 (I don't go — by choice / I'm not going), 안 먹어요 (I don't eat). Note the difference: 못 먹어요 = can't eat (circumstance prevents it), 안 먹어요 = won't/don't eat (volition).

-고 있다 (Present Progressive): Verb stem + 고 있다 = is/am/are -ing. Examples: 먹고 있어요 (I'm eating), 공부하고 있어요 (I'm studying), 자고 있어요 (I'm sleeping). Past progressive: 고 있었다: 어제 3시에 공부하고 있었어요 (I was studying at 3 yesterday). Can also mean the state of wearing: 안경을 쓰고 있어요 (I'm wearing glasses — not 'putting on').

-아/어 주다 (Do as a Favor): Verb stem + 아/어 주다 = doing something for someone. Examples: 문을 열어 주세요 (Please open the door for me), 설명해 주세요 (Please explain), 도와 주세요 (Please help). With 드리다 (honorific) instead of 주다: 할머니께 읽어 드렸어요 (I read it for grandmother). Very commonly used for requests and offers of help.

-고 싶다 (Want to): Verb stem + 고 싶다 = want to do. Examples: 먹고 싶어요 (I want to eat), 가고 싶어요 (I want to go), 배우고 싶어요 (I want to learn). Past: 가고 싶었어요 (I wanted to go). Asking: 뭐 먹고 싶어요? (What do you want to eat?). For third-person, use 고 싶어하다: 친구가 한국에 가고 싶어해요 (My friend wants to go to Korea).

-아/어야 되다/하다 (Must/Have to): Verb stem + 아/어야 되다/하다 = must, have to. Examples: 공부해야 돼요 (I have to study), 일찍 일어나야 해요 (I must wake up early). Past: 가야 했어요 (I had to go). Negative (don't have to): -지 않아도 되다: 안 가도 돼요 (You don't have to go). Prohibition (must not): -(으)면 안 되다: 여기서 담배를 피우면 안 돼요 (You must not smoke here).

Korean Honorifics: Korean has a complex honorific system. -(으)시- infix adds respect to the subject's action: 가다 → 가시다 (go — honorific), 먹다 → 드시다 (eat — irregular honorific), 자다 → 주무시다 (sleep — irregular), 말하다 → 말씀하시다 (speak — irregular). Honorific subject particle: 께서. Add -요 or -습니다 for listener respect. 주다 → 드리다 (give — humble form for when giving to superior). Use formal speech with strangers and superiors; use 반말 (casual) only with close friends, children, and those younger.

-(으)면 (If/When): Verb stem + (으)면 = if/when. 으면 after consonant (except ㄹ): 먹으면 (if I eat), 읽으면 (if I read). 면 after vowel and ㄹ: 가면 (if I go), 만들면 (if I make) — ㄹ dropped. Examples: 비가 오면 집에 있을 거예요 (If it rains, I'll stay home), 시간이 있으면 전화해 주세요 (If you have time, please call me). Past conditional: -(으)면 좋겠다 (I wish): 비가 왔으면 좋겠어요 (I wish it would rain).

-지만 (But/Although): Verb stem + 지만 = but/although. Examples: 맛있지만 비싸요 (It's delicious but expensive), 한국어는 어렵지만 재미있어요 (Korean is difficult but interesting), 피곤하지만 공부해야 돼요 (I'm tired but I have to study). Can be attached to adjectives too: 춥지만 괜찮아요 (It's cold but it's okay).

-(으)ㄹ 수 있다/없다 (Can/Cannot): Verb stem + (으)ㄹ 수 있다 = can (have ability/possibility), 을/ㄹ 수 없다 = cannot. Examples: 한국어를 할 수 있어요? (Can you speak Korean?), 수영할 수 있어요 (I can swim), 오늘 못 갈 수 있어요 (I might not be able to go today — possibility, not just ability). Past: 갈 수 있었어요 (I could go / was able to go). Future: 갈 수 있을 거예요 (I will be able to go).

~(으)면서 (While/At the same time): Verb stem + (으)면서 = while doing, at the same time as. Examples: 음악을 들으면서 공부해요 (I study while listening to music), 밥을 먹으면서 TV를 봐요 (I watch TV while eating), 걸으면서 이야기해요 (Let's talk while walking). Can express contradictory situations: 모르면서 아는 척해요 (He pretends to know while not knowing).

~(으)ㄴ/는 것 (Nominalization — Turning Verbs into Nouns): Makes a verb clause into a noun. Present: Verb stem + 는 것: 먹는 것 (eating/the act of eating), 가는 것 (going). Past: Verb stem + (으)ㄴ 것: 먹은 것 (what I ate/the fact that I ate), 본 것 (what I saw). Future: Verb stem + (으)ㄹ 것: 먹을 것 (what I will eat). Examples: 운동하는 것을 좋아해요 (I like exercising), 어제 본 것을 기억해요 (I remember what I saw yesterday). 것 → 거 in casual speech: 먹는 거 좋아해요.

~(으)ㄴ/는데 (Background/Contrast Connector): Provides background information or mild contrast between clauses. Verb stem + 는데: 가는데 (going, but...), 먹는데 (eating, and...). Adjective stem + (으)ㄴ데: 예쁜데 (pretty, but...), 추운데 (cold, so...). Past: 았/었는데: 갔는데 (went, but...). Examples: 한국어를 배우는데 정말 재미있어요 (I'm learning Korean, and it's really interesting), 비싼데 안 살래요 (It's expensive, so I won't buy it), 어제 갔었는데 문이 닫혀 있었어요 (I went yesterday, but the door was closed).

~(으)니까 (Because/Since): Verb stem + (으)니까 = because, since (emphasizing reason, often with personal experience/discovery). Examples: 배가 고프니까 밥을 먹자 (Since I'm hungry, let's eat), 비가 오니까 우산을 가져가세요 (Since it's raining, take an umbrella). Difference from 아/어서: (으)니까 can be used with commands/suggestions, while 아/어서 cannot (it's more neutral). Future/past: 비가 올 거니까 (since it will rain), 배가 고팠으니까 (since I was hungry).

Double Past Tense (-았/었었-): Expresses a completed past action that no longer holds true. Examples: 한국에 살았었어요 (I used to live in Korea — but don't anymore), 그 영화를 봤었어요 (I had seen that movie — at some point before), 그 사람을 알았었어요 (I used to know that person). Different from simple past: 살았어요 = I lived (neutral past), 살았었어요 = I used to live (but not anymore).

Quoting in Korean: Use -다고/-라고/-자고/-냐고 for reported speech. Statement: Verb + (ㄴ/는)다고 (하다): 간다고 했어요 (She said she's going), 먹는다고 했어요 (He said he's eating). Adjective + 다고: 예쁘다고 했어요 (She said it's pretty). Noun + (이)라고: 학생이라고 했어요 (He said he's a student). Command: -(으)라고: 가라고 했어요 (She told me to go). Suggestion: -자고: 같이 가자고 했어요 (She suggested going together). Question: -(느)냐고: 뭐 하냐고 물어봤어요 (He asked what I was doing).

Passive and Causative Forms: Passive (피동): Add -이/히/리/기 to verb stem: 보다 → 보이다 (to be seen), 먹다 → 먹히다 (to be eaten), 열다 → 열리다 (to be opened), 듣다 → 들리다 (to be heard). Causative (사동): Add -이/히/리/기/우/구/추: 먹다 → 먹이다 (to feed), 앉다 → 앉히다 (to seat someone), 보다 → 보이다 (to show). Sometimes same suffix works for both: 보이다 can mean 'be seen' or 'show'. Context determines meaning.

~더라고요 (Recalling Past Experience): Expresses something the speaker personally observed or experienced in the past and is now recalling. Verb stem + 더라고요. Examples: 그 식당 음식이 맛있더라고요 (I found the food at that restaurant delicious — from my experience), 한국에 갔을 때 날씨가 정말 춥더라고요 (When I went to Korea, I found the weather really cold). Difference from simple past: 맛있었어요 (It was delicious — factual), 맛있더라고요 (I personally experienced/found it delicious — personal recollection).
